---
title: Smoked brisket with oak
date: 2024-05-30
tags: [cooking, woodworking, smoking, bbq]
---

A whole packer brisket is two muscles joined by a seam of fat, and learning to read that geometry was half the battle. I trim the hard fat down to a quarter inch, leave the deckle intact, and rub the meat generously with equal parts kosher salt and coarse pepper the night before.

For fuel I prefer white oak split from offcuts of past furniture projects. It burns clean with a gentle, almost sweet smoke that does not overpower beef the way hickory can. The fire sits at around two-fifty, and a water pan keeps the chamber humid during the long stall.

Wrapping in butcher paper around the stall shortens the cook without sacrificing bark. I pull the brisket when a probe slides in like warm butter, usually around two hundred and three degrees, then rest it in a cooler for at least an hour before slicing against the grain.
